title: Backend Load Remediation - Map Query Guardrails
scope: backend map endpoints and map DAO query shaping
problem: Several map endpoints allow large result sets and perform broad DB reads followed by Python-side filtering, which can amplify DB latency, API memory use, and response serialization cost under concurrent traffic.
risk: Large bbox or high-limit requests can cause expensive scans, oversized payloads, and noisy-neighbor effects on the PostgreSQL pool and FastAPI workers.

evidence.note: /map/nearby-bbox converts bbox to radius, fetches a broader candidate set, and then filters in Python.
/map/nearby accepts caller-provided limit without a visible upper bound.
/map/stores-by-dong returns up to 1500 rows for one click path.
Nearby store lookup uses only bounding-box predicates and LIMIT, without ordering by distance or tighter SQL-side constraints.
Dong/building queries can return large row sets and rely on generic fetch-all behavior.

goals: Bound worst-case query cost for public or semi-public map reads.
Move as much filtering and trimming as practical into SQL.
Keep response sizes predictable enough for frontend rendering and gzip.

implementation_plan.action: Add explicit FastAPI parameter validation for map read endpoints.
details: Use Query constraints such as ge/le for radius and limit on /map/nearby and /map/nearby-bbox.
Choose conservative defaults and hard caps, for example nearby <= 200 and bbox <= 300, unless current product UX proves a higher number is required.
Reject obviously abusive bbox dimensions before hitting the DAO.

implementation_plan.action: Replace Python-side bbox filtering with SQL-side bbox predicates.
details: Add a dedicated DAO method that queries store_seoul with min/max lng/lat directly.
Keep a strict LIMIT in SQL and consider ordering by approximate distance only if needed by UX.
Return only the already-bounded SQL result rather than a larger candidate set.

implementation_plan.action: Reduce default payload size for dong and building lookups.
details: Review whether 1500 rows for /map/stores-by-dong is actually needed in one response.
If the frontend only renders markers for visible or summarized subsets, lower the hard cap and optionally return a truncation flag.
Consider splitting summary and detail endpoints if one endpoint is serving both use cases.

implementation_plan.action: Add observability for expensive map reads.
details: Log request parameters and response counts for capped queries.
Emit warnings when requests hit caps so product can detect legitimate overflow cases.

acceptance_criteria: Map endpoints enforce documented upper bounds on limit and spatial range.
/map/nearby-bbox no longer fetches a broad candidate set only to filter it in Python.
Worst-case map response size is materially lower and predictable.
Tests cover cap enforcement and representative bbox behavior.
verification: Run backend tests touching map routers and DAO behavior.
Add or update unit tests for parameter cap validation.
Smoke-test representative nearby, bbox, and dong flows from the frontend contract.
execution_notes_for_claude_code: Preserve current response schema unless a truncation indicator is added intentionally.
If exact frontend dependencies on current row counts are unclear, implement hard caps with minimal schema change first and document the chosen thresholds.
